news_headline: Cybersecurity Researchers Flag Critical CI/CD Flaw 'Cordyceps' Exposing Repos at Microsoft, Google, Apache, and Cloudflare
post_text:
A security firm just proved that any unauthenticated user — with nothing but a free account — could forge approvals, push malicious code, or steal credentials across repositories at Microsoft, Google, Apache, and Cloudflare. No special access required.

The vulnerability, dubbed Cordyceps, was caught by researchers at Novee Security before it could be weaponized. That's not luck. That's ethical hacking working exactly as intended.

Back in 2021, our writers at the Waterloo Business Review examined why companies were increasingly paying people to break their own systems — and how that redraws the incentive structures of the entire security industry. The Cordyceps disclosure is a live demonstration of that argument: the most valuable thing a firm can buy isn't a stronger lock, it's someone trying to pick it first.

As attack surfaces grow and AI accelerates exploitation timelines, that calculus is only becoming more urgent. The business case for ethical hacking isn't niche anymore — it's foundational.

Worth a read if you haven't seen it. Link in the comments.

#Cybersecurity #EthicalHacking

The vulnerability, dubbed Cordyceps, was caught by researchers at Novee Security before it could be weaponized. That's not luck. That's ethical hacking working exactly as intended.

Back in 2021, our writers at the Waterloo Business Review examined why companies were increasingly paying people to break their own systems — and how that redraws the incentive structures of the entire security industry. The Cordyceps disclosure is a live demonstration of that argument: the most valuable thing a firm can buy isn't a stronger lock, it's someone trying to pick it first.

As attack surfaces grow and AI accelerates exploitation timelines, that calculus is only becoming more urgent. The business case for ethical hacking isn't niche anymore — it's foundational.
